metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the adjudication corpus artifact schema: specify the exact value vocabulary for the 'adjudication' column (e.g. enum {"block-worthy", "not-block-worthy"}), the required 'evidence' field format, and the file format (e.g. JSONL with named fields), co-located with AC1 or in the derivation script's docstring.
findings.finding: The adjudication corpus artifact schema is referenced but its value vocabulary is never defined. AC1 requires columns '(finding id, impact, adjudication, evidence)' committed under docs/experiments/, but the plan never specifies: (a) what values the 'adjudication' field takes (a boolean? the enum {"block-worthy", "not-block-worthy"}? {"YES", "NO", "UNCERTAIN"}?), (b) what format 'evidence' must be (a URL, a free-text sentence, a structured dict?), or (c) what file format the artifact uses (JSONL, CSV, Markdown table). The existing fixture code_review_impact_labels.jsonl uses {label: HIGH/NIT, note, severity_attributes} — a different schema — so the new derivation script has no canonical column contract to implement against.
findings.location: What section, step 1 ('Adjudicate each finding...'); AC1 ('The adjudicated corpus (finding id, impact, adjudication, evidence) is committed under docs/experiments/')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section or AC1: 'Adjudication corpus schema (JSONL, one record per finding): {"finding_id": str, "impact": float, "adjudication": "block-worthy" | "not-block-worthy", "evidence": str (URL or free-text rationale)}.' Mirror the existing fixture's structure so the derivation script can share a loader.

findings.finding: AC3 misdescribes the ADR-0036 A/B gate as 'HIGH<->NIT separation vs baseline', but `ab_impact_model.py` is explicit that the baseline score 'does NOT gate' and the actual gate is an ABSOLUTE criterion: `separation > 0.30 AND median_nit < 0.30`. The baseline ('old mean impact') is printed 'for transparency' only. An executor checking AC3 against the script output could incorrectly treat a baseline-beating result as a pass when the absolute criterion fails.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'The ADR-0036 A/B gate result (HIGH<->NIT separation vs baseline) is recorded and passes.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(HIGH<->NIT separation vs baseline)' with '(absolute: `median(HIGH)−median(NIT) > 0.30` AND `median(NIT) < 0.30`; baseline is reported for transparency but does NOT gate — see `ab_impact_model.py`)' in AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'where feasible' with a stated default procedure: e.g. 'strip the impact field from sidecar copies before adjudication; if automated stripping is impractical, record the adjudicator's knowledge state and note it in the corpus artifact'.
findings.finding: The adjudication step says 'blind to its score where feasible' but defers the blinding decision entirely to the executor, leaving the methodology open-ended when blinding is deemed infeasible.
findings.location: What – second bullet: 'Adjudicate each finding with impact >= 0.3 as block-worthy vs not (blind to its score where feasible)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'Adjudicate each finding (impact >= 0.3) as block-worthy vs not. Default blinding procedure: copy sidecars to a working file with the `impact` field removed before adjudication; if impact is unavoidable (e.g. visible in commit message), note the adjudicator's awareness in the evidence column of the corpus artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete data source and tooling path for 'post-merge history (follow-up fix commits, red-main incidents)' — e.g., a named git log query against the main branch, a CI system endpoint, or a manual annotation protocol — so the adjudication join step is implementable without improvisation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a join to 'post-merge history (follow-up fix commits, red-main incidents)' as part of assembling the corpus, but no tooling path, API surface, or data source for 'red-main incidents' is specified or exists in the codebase — this is an unverified capability for a step the adjudication methodology depends on.
findings.location: What section: 'Join to their Gerrit changes and post-merge history (follow-up fix commits, red-main incidents)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the vague join description with a concrete protocol: e.g., 'For each finding with a `change_id`, run `git log --oneline <merge_sha>..HEAD -- <cited_paths>` on the main branch to identify follow-up fix commits; classify as a confirmed must-fix if a fix commit appears within N days of merge. Red-main incidents are annotated manually from the on-call log (link to log source). Document which of the 3 named cases (#529, #518, #539) required this join and what source was used.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the acceptance criterion for Sonnet-scored simulation: define the agreement metric and threshold vs. held-out manual adjudication (e.g., accuracy >= 80% on held-out slice), the minimum held-out sample size, and a fallback (e.g., expand manual adjudication) if the simulation fails the acceptance threshold.
findings.finding: The 'Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale' path names a two-stage protocol (automated Sonnet scoring + held-out manual adjudication for validation), but provides no agreement threshold below which the simulation is rejected, no minimum held-out sample size, and no fallback if Sonnet labels diverge from manual labels. This leaves the executor unable to determine when the simulated adjudication is acceptable.
findings.location: What section, step 2: 'manual scoring of the would-block slice; Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale'

findings.finding: The plan references 'Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale' as the adjudication methodology, but the protocol is never specified: no prompt schema, no definition of what the model is asked to decide, no vocabulary for the adjudication label (block-worthy vs not?), no inter-rater agreement threshold, and no description of how the held-out human adjudication set is constructed to validate the Sonnet labels. The decision rule used during adjudication is not co-located with the adjudication corpus schema (AC1).
findings.location: What section, bullet 2 ('Adjudicate each finding with impact >= 0.3 as block-worthy vs not (blind to its score where feasible), mirroring the b463 plan-review methodology (manual scoring of the would-block slice; Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recall measurement alongside precision in the threshold derivation: for each candidate t, report both precision(block-worthy | impact >= t) AND recall(impact >= t | block-worthy), and state the minimum acceptable recall as part of the selection criterion; also clarify whether #529 (impact 0.0) is treated as a threshold gap or a model gap.
findings.finding: The plan selects thresholds using a precision-only criterion (smallest t where precision > 0.5, tie-broken toward fewer false blocks), but the ticket's own motivation is recall failures: three confirmed must-fix findings (#529 impact 0.0, #518 at 0.54, #539 at 0.60) were NOT blocked. A threshold optimized solely for precision minimizes false blocks but provides no floor on recall — it could systematically under-block findings of the type that motivated this work. The plan gives no positive rationale for why precision > 0.5 is the right decision criterion rather than, e.g., precision > 0.5 AND recall > some minimum, or an F-measure.
findings.location: What section: 'select the SMALLEST t where precision > 0.5, tie-broken toward fewer false blocks'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the blinding protocol precisely: state what fields are hidden from the adjudicator (at minimum: the scalar impact score; ideally also the consequence binary attributes), how blinding is enforced in the derivation script (e.g., a separate blinded export step that strips numeric fields before adjudication), and whether Sonnet-scored simulation is also blinded to the scalar.
findings.finding: The blinding protocol for adjudication is underspecified. The plan says 'adjudicate each finding with impact >= 0.3 as block-worthy vs not (blind to its score where feasible).' The code-review sidecar stores both the scalar impact score AND the consequence binary attributes (blocking/advisory buckets with full severity_attributes). An adjudicator reading the finding text will also see the binary attributes, which are the direct inputs to impact_code — being 'blind to the scalar score' while seeing the binary inputs that determine it provides weak blinding and could systematically bias adjudication to match the model's own outputs, invalidating the independence of the ground truth.
findings.location: What section: 'Adjudicate each finding with impact >= 0.3 as block-worthy vs not (blind to its score where feasible)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the derivation script, add a `--export-for-adjudication` mode that writes finding text + post-merge evidence only (strips `impact`, `severity_attributes`, and bucket assignment) to a separate file; adjudicators label from that file, then labels are joined back by finding id before precision is computed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the blinding protocol for the adjudication step: specify how the impact score is hidden from the adjudicator (e.g., strip 'impact' field from the adjudication worksheet), what 'feasible' means operationally, and what fallback applies when full blinding is infeasible (e.g., two-reviewer reconciliation, or record that adjudication was unblinded as a limitation).
findings.finding: The 'blind to its score where feasible' blinding protocol is invoked as a methodological requirement but never defined — no process for enforcing blindness (who sees what, in what order), no operational definition of 'feasible', and no fallback when blinding is declared infeasible. This is a pink-elephant antipattern: naming a constraint without specifying how to satisfy or relax it.
findings.location: What section, step 2: 'Adjudicate each finding with impact >= 0.3 as block-worthy vs not (blind to its score where feasible)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the logical relationship between the A/B gate result and the precision@threshold derivation: specify (a) whether the gate is expected to be newly passing for this story or is a regression check, and (b) what the decision rule is if the gate passes but no candidate threshold achieves precision > 0.5 in the field corpus (or if gate fails but a threshold clearly does).
findings.finding: The A/B gate (ab_impact_model.py) measures HIGH↔NIT median separation on a static pre-labeled fixture — it answers 'does the model discriminate at all?' but does NOT validate whether precision > 0.5 holds at any specific candidate threshold in the field corpus. The plan treats passing the A/B gate as a necessary prerequisite ('mandated evidence gate before any threshold-down') that is logically independent from the threshold precision derivation. A well-separating model can still have precision < 0.5 at every candidate threshold if the field distribution is skewed; conversely, the gate likely already passes today (test_impact_code.py asserts the same separation contract on the same fixture). Mandating the gate without clarifying how its result integrates with — or could override — the precision derivation leaves a logical gap in the decision rule.
findings.location: What section: 'Run docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py … as the mandated evidence gate before any threshold-down'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or bound the 'Sonnet-scored simulation' adjudication path against the established manual adjudication approach: either cite prior art for LLM self-adjudication of threshold derivation, define the held-out validation mechanism explicitly, or restrict the simulation path to a secondary/sensitivity analysis with human adjudication as the primary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces 'Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale' as an alternative adjudication methodology for determining block-worthiness of findings, but provides no prior-art justification for using an LLM to adjudicate findings produced by the same LLM-based review system. This is a novel pattern: the b463 plan-review precedent the plan explicitly cites used manual/interactive adjudication. Using Sonnet to score whether Sonnet's own code-review findings are block-worthy introduces a circularity risk that is not acknowledged or bounded.
findings.location: What section / 'Sonnet-scored simulation with held-out adjudication acceptable for scale'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 0.60-ceiling analysis, explicitly distinguish between a model ceiling (impossible to score > 0.60) and a corpus artifact (no serious-tier binary combinations observed yet); compute the theoretical maximum impact_code value and note which binary combinations would produce scores above 0.60 to make the analysis future-proof.
findings.finding: The plan's '0.60 ceiling' analysis treats the observed field-distribution ceiling as a model property, but from the codebase it is a corpus artifact: `_CODE_REVERSIBILITY_FLOOR = 0.6` in decide.py floors any finding with `impact_base > 0 AND hard_to_reverse_surface` to exactly 0.60. Serious production-lane binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere) can reach 0.72–0.90 but have not yet appeared in the 149-record field corpus. If the ceiling is a corpus artifact and future reviews do emit serious-tier binaries, the threshold derived under this analysis (anchored at t=0.6) will suddenly encompass a much wider population of findings without re-derivation, invalidating the precision guarantee.
findings.location: What section: 'whether the 0.60 ceiling means t=0.6 only ever catches max-impact findings'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the derivation script and the written recommendation: 'The 0.60 ceiling in the field corpus is a corpus artifact — the reversibility floor (decide.py _CODE_REVERSIBILITY_FLOOR=0.6) creates a cluster AT 0.60, but serious-tier binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere) can produce scores up to 0.90. The threshold derivation should report what fraction of the theoretical score range [0.60, 0.90] has been observed in the corpus.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the threshold conclusion (chosen t, precision table, 0.54-band and 0.60-ceiling analyses) as a section in the committed docs/experiments/ artifact (e.g., a `FINDINGS.md` or as a structured comment block at the top of the derivation script) rather than solely in a ticket comment.
findings.finding: The chosen threshold, precision-crossover justification, and 0.54/0.60-band analyses are captured only in a ticket comment — not in the committed docs/experiments/ artifact — making the decision rationale non-discoverable from the codebase when future maintainers re-run calibration or implement the sibling enablement story.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The chosen threshold and its precision-crossover justification are recorded in a comment on this ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'The chosen threshold and supporting precision table are written as a `## Conclusion` section in a committed `docs/experiments/code_review_threshold_derivation.md` (or equivalent) file, with the ticket reference included for traceability.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the newly adjudicated corpus rows should be appended to `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` (or passed via `--fixture` to `ab_impact_model.py`) before AC3 is checked, so the A/B gate is exercised on the 149-finding code-v2 corpus rather than only the pre-existing 31-entry fixture.
findings.finding: The plan instructs running `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` as the 'mandated evidence gate before any threshold-down', but that script scores only the EXISTING `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture (~31 entries seeded from prior adjudication). The ~149 newly adjudicated findings from the code-v2 corpus are NOT wired into that fixture. The A/B gate therefore passes or fails on the pre-existing labeled set, not on the new corpus — making AC3 ('the ADR-0036 A/B gate result … passes') potentially misleading: it verifies the model separates the fixture, not that it separates the newly collected findings.
findings.location: What section / DERIVATION TOOLING paragraph; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step in DERIVATION TOOLING: 'After adjudication, add the adjudicated HIGH/NIT rows to `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` (or pass the new corpus JSONL as `--fixture docs/experiments/<corpus>.jsonl`) so the A/B gate in AC3 verifies separation on the code-v2 field corpus, not just the seeded fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the '0.60 ceiling' claim: verify whether impact_code scores above 0.60 are absent from the post-2026-07-08 corpus due to DET-enrichment gaps, a short observation window, or sampling, and document the explanation in the experiment artifact — the threshold analysis must account for the full theoretical range (up to 0.9).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an apparent 0.60 ceiling' on observed impact scores and uses this as a load-bearing premise for the threshold analysis, but impact_code is demonstrably capable of scores up to 0.9 (e.g., data_loss_without_recovery=True + silent_failure=True yields 0.9 per test_impact_code.py; data_loss_without_recovery alone yields 0.72). The 0.60 ceiling is an unverified empirical claim about the corpus that contradicts the model's known theoretical range, and the plan does not explain whether it arises from a DET-enrichment gap, a short post-2026-07-08 window, or another artifact.
findings.location: Why section / 'Observed impact distribution clusters at {0, 0.32, 0.36, 0.54, 0.60} with an apparent 0.60 ceiling'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a corpus-characterization step to derive_code_review_threshold.py that reports the score distribution histogram (including checking for the presence or absence of scores > 0.60) and cross-checks against the model's theoretical max (from ab_impact_model.py fixture rows), before computing precision at each candidate threshold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a quantitative pass criterion to AC3 (e.g. 'ab_impact_model.py exits 0 and reports HIGH/NIT AUC separation >= the ADR-0036 baseline') so the gate outcome is deterministic and repo-verifiable.
findings.finding: AC3 states the A/B gate 'passes' but neither the AC nor the plan body defines what metric or output value constitutes passing for ab_impact_model.py — an executor cannot determine done-ness without knowing the pass criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'The ADR-0036 A/B gate result (HIGH<->NIT separation vs baseline) is recorded and passes.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC3: 'The ADR-0036 A/B gate (`python docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py`) exits 0 and reports HIGH↔NIT separation metric >= the pre-change baseline recorded in docs/experiments/; result is committed as an artifact under docs/experiments/ab_gate_result.json.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC3 with the Scope: either change 'is recorded and passes' to 'is recorded' (with the pass requirement moving to the sibling enablement story), or add an explicit note that fixing a gate failure IS in scope for this ticket even though no config change is made.
findings.finding: AC3 requires the ADR-0036 A/B gate to 'pass' as a hard acceptance criterion on this ticket, but the Scope explicitly excludes any threshold change ('NO routing/config change here — that is the sibling enablement story'). The What section frames this gate as a prerequisite 'before any threshold-down', which belongs to the sibling enablement story. Requiring the gate to pass (not merely be recorded) on an analysis-only ticket conflates the two stories: if the gate fails for unrelated reasons, the analysis deliverable — which is complete and correct — cannot be accepted.
findings.location: Scope section ('NO routing/config change here') vs AC3 ('is recorded and passes')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'The ADR-0036 A/B gate result (HIGH<->NIT separation vs baseline) is recorded; a failing result must be investigated and resolved before the sibling enablement story proceeds.' Remove 'and passes' as a hard AC on this ticket.

findings.finding: `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` reads plan-review-specific sidecar fields (`data.get('findings', [])` and `data.get('material_fingerprint')`) that are ABSENT from code-review sidecars (`code_review_result_v1`), which store findings in `blocking`/`advisory` buckets and use `change_fingerprint` instead. Running this script with `--impact-model-version code-v2` against the code-review corpus will silently yield zero findings and no revision-response signal, producing a vacuous calibration result — yet the plan names it as the 'mandated evidence gate before any threshold-down'.
findings.location: ## What (step 4): 'Run docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py + the version-segmented replay (docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2) per ADR 0036'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) note that a custom derivation script/notebook is needed (not `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`) and commit it under `docs/experiments/` as the AC1 artifact, or (b) extend `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` to handle the `code_review_result_v1` schema (merging `blocking`+`advisory` into its `findings` accumulator and using `change_fingerprint` as the revision key). The plan's AC1 should name whichever artifact will actually run.

findings.finding: The mandated `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2` replay will extract ZERO findings from code-review sidecars because the tool reads `data.get('findings', [])` (plan-review sidecar schema) while code-review `build_payload` writes findings into separate `blocking`/`advisory`/`coaching` keys — not a `findings` key. The ADR-0036 evidence gate will silently produce an empty corpus table rather than failing loudly, making the A/B + segmented-replay AC unverifiable.
findings.location: What section — 'Run docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py + the version-segmented replay (docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2)'; also AC line 3 ('The ADR-0036 A/B gate result … is recorded and passes.').
findings.suggested_fix: Add an extraction step (script or notebook) that reads `.tickets-tracker/*/*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` sidecars whose `data.schema == 'code_review_result_v1'` and `data.impact_model_version == 'code-v2'`, flattens `blocking + advisory` into a finding list (each carrying `criteria`, `impact`, `validity`, `priority`), and either (a) feeds that list directly to the precision-sweep computation or (b) rewrites it into the plan-review sidecar shape so `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` can consume it.

findings.finding: calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py was built for plan-review sidecars: its automated-proposal logic keys on plan-review criterion IDs (G6, COH, T5e, etc.) and its revision-response signal measures material_fingerprint deltas between consecutive plan-review rounds of the SAME ticket — a signal that does not exist for code-review, where revisions are Gerrit patch-sets, not ticket fingerprint changes. Pointing this script at code-review sidecars (--impact-model-version code-v2) will produce per-criterion fire-rate / validity rows, but (a) the criterion labels will be code-review criterion IDs the script has never been validated against, and (b) the revision-response column (rev_rr) will read ~0 for all criteria because code-review tickets do not go through repeated plan-review cycles with material_fingerprint changes. The plan treats this script's output as a sufficient ADR-0036 evidence gate without noting or bridging this structural mismatch.
findings.location: What section — 'Run docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py + the version-segmented replay (docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2) per ADR 0036'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the AC/What section acknowledging that rev_rr will be ~0 for code-review criteria (no plan-revision loop), and specify that the ADR-0036 evidence gate will rely on the fire-rate, validity, and priority-percentile columns only — explicitly excluding the revision-response column as inapplicable to code-review.

findings.finding: The plan mandates running `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2` over code-review REVIEW_RESULT sidecars, but the tool reads `data.get('findings', [])` while the code-review sidecar schema stores findings under `blocking`, `advisory`, and `coaching` keys — not `findings`. This is a proven schema mismatch: the tool will extract zero findings from every code-review sidecar, silently producing empty per-criterion tables.
findings.location: What section / AC5 ('Run docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add an explicit step: 'Pre-process code-v2 REVIEW_RESULT sidecars to normalise findings: merge `blocking + advisory` lists into a `findings`-keyed structure (with a `decision` field set accordingly) before passing to `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`, or extend the tool's `load()` function to handle both schemas.'

findings.finding: calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py's revision-response signal (rev_rr) is structurally inapplicable to code-review sidecars: it measures material_fingerprint deltas between consecutive REVIEW_RESULT rounds of the SAME ticket — a plan-review-only concept. Code-review artifacts are keyed on Gerrit change IDs and do not accumulate fingerprint-changing re-review rounds per ticket. Running --impact-model-version code-v2 will silently yield rev_rr ≈ 0 for all criteria, and the script's BLOCK-ELIGIBLE branch gates on rev_rr >= 0.6 — so no code-review criterion will ever be classified as block-eligible by this tool. The plan presents this run as the mandated ADR-0036 evidence gate without noting or bridging this structural mismatch.
findings.location: What section — 'Run docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version code-v2 per ADR 0036'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan note: 'The rev_rr column from calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py is not meaningful for code-review sidecars (no fingerprint-round accumulation); the ADR-0036 gate for this story relies solely on ab_impact_model.py (HIGH↔NIT separation) and the precision(block-worthy | impact >= t) table from the new derivation script. The calibrate script is run only for fire-rate and validity columns.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
